codeReview.summary: Clean, well-structured implementation of Codex device code flow as an alternative login method. The code follows existing patterns (reuses DeviceCodeError type, parseTokenResponse, TokenStore, device_code.html template). All 12 unit tests and 3 integration tests pass, go vet is clean, and the full test suite succeeds.

issues.description: pollForToken does not enforce a client-side deadline based on the device code's ExpiresIn field. The polling loop only checks ctx.Done(), but in InitiateDeviceCodeLogin (codex.go:1057), context.Background() is used, meaning the goroutine will poll indefinitely until the server returns expired_token. This is not a bug since the server should enforce expiry, but adding a client-side timeout would be more robust. For example: ctx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(ctx, time.Duration(dcResp.ExpiresIn)*time.Second).

The subtitle says 'Authorize using GitHub Device Code Flow' but this template is reused for Codex/OpenAI device code flows too. The text should say 'Authorize using Device Code Flow' or be parameterized by backend type.

InitiateDeviceCodeLogin starts a background goroutine with context.Background() that polls until authorization completes or the device code expires (server-side). If the proxy restarts, the goroutine is lost and the pending device code flow is abandoned with no feedback to the user. This is acceptable for an MVP but worth noting for future improvement (e.g., persisting pending flows).

sharedStateObservations.observation: The Codex device code flow endpoint (POST auth.openai.com/oauth/device/code) and its polling URL (auth.openai.com/oauth/token with grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code) are not documented in .factory/library/. The existing codex-backend.md only covers the PKCE flow. The device code flow constants (client_id, device code URL, default poll interval, default expiry) should be recorded.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/oauth/codex_device_code.go:18-30 defines these constants. .factory/library/codex-backend.md doesn't mention device code flow.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The pattern of returning JSON-encoded DeviceCodeLoginInfo as the authURL string from InitiateLogin/InitiateDeviceCodeLogin is used by both CopilotBackend and CodexBackend but isn't documented in AGENTS.md or architecture.md as a convention. The web handler checks for this JSON by attempting to unmarshal it and checking UserCode != ''. This is an implicit contract between backend and web layers.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: internal/web/web.go:879-881 — the web handler parses authURL as JSON to detect device code flow vs redirect flow. This pattern is used by both CopilotBackend.InitiateLogin and CodexBackend.InitiateDeviceCodeLogin.

summary: PASS. The Codex device code flow implementation is thorough and well-tested. It correctly implements the full device code OAuth flow: POST to auth.openai.com/oauth/device/code with the correct client_id, displays user_code and verification_uri in the web UI, polls auth.openai.com/oauth/token with the device_code grant type, handles all error cases (authorization_pending, slow_down, expired_token, access_denied), and stores tokens with the same lifecycle as PKCE tokens. The authorize URL includes all required parameters (codex_cli_simplified_flow=true, id_token_add_organizations=true, originator=codex_cli_rs). Web UI properly shows both 'Connect (Browser)' and 'Connect (Device Code)' buttons for Codex backends. 12 unit tests and 3 integration tests cover initiation, polling, error handling, token persistence, token refresh compatibility, concurrent access, context cancellation, and full end-to-end flow. Three minor non-blocking issues noted: no client-side expiry enforcement in polling, template subtitle says 'GitHub' but is used for OpenAI too, and background polling goroutine doesn't survive restarts.
